elements.sent-2411-001: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-Training (CLIP) is highly instrumental in machine learning applications within a large variety of domains.
elements.sent-2411-002: We investigate the geometry of this embedding, which is still not well understood.
elements.sent-2411-003: We examine the raw unnormalized embedding and show that text and image reside on linearly separable ellipsoid shells, not centered at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-004: We explain the benefits of having this structure, allowing to better embed instances according to their uncertainty during contrastive training.
elements.sent-2411-005: Frequent concepts in the dataset yield more false negatives, inducing greater uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-006: A new notion of conformity is introduced, which measures the average cosine similarity of an instance to any other instance within a representative data set.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show this measure can be accurately estimated by simply computing the cosine similarity to the modality mean vector.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, we find that CLIP's modality gap optimizes the matching of the conformity distributions of image and text.
elements.sent-2411-009: Multi-modal approaches, particularly Contrastive Language-Image Pre-Training (CLIP) \cite{radford2021learning , have significantly advanced various computer vision tasks, enabling applications that were hard to imagine just a few years ago.}
elements.sent-2411-010: These advancements include generating high-quality images from user prompts \cite{ramesh2022hierarchical, nichol2021glide ; performing open-vocabulary classification \cite{he2023open , segmentation \cite{liang2023open, yu2024convolutions and detection \cite{wu2023cora ; captioning \cite{mokady2021clipcap, cho2022fine , and even semantic editing \cite{kim2022diffusionclip, kawar2023imagic .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Much of this success can be attributed to the key advancements made in multi-modal learning, with CLIP playing a key role.
elements.sent-2411-012: Following the remarkable success of vision-language models (VLMs), a wide range of adaptations has emerged for additional domains such as 3D \cite{hegde2023clip, chen2023clip2scene, zhang2022pointclip , video \cite{tang2021clip4caption, luo2022clip4clip and audio \cite{wu2022wav2clip, guzhov2022audioclip .}}}

This allows to control uncertainty in contrastive learning, where as themes become more rare (lower uncertainty) they reside farther from the mean modality vector. } \label{fig:geom}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite the practical advances, the structure of CLIP's latent space is not fully understood.
elements.sent-2411-014: While properties such as alignment, uniformity, and the well-studied modality gap have been explored, they do not fully capture the geometry underlying this multi-modal space.

elements.sent-2411-017: Since projection is an information reducing operation which ``flattens'' the data, we examine the raw embedding, before normalization to a unit norm.
elements.sent-2411-018: We show that CLIP’s raw latent space exhibits a double-ellipsoid geometry, one for text and one for images.
elements.sent-2411-019: Both ellipsoids are shifted from the origin (see \cref{fig:geom ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: The offset from the origin explains the narrow cone effect, while their separability explains the modality gap phenomenon \cite{liang2022mind, fahim2024its, schrodi2024two .}
elements.sent-2411-021: We perform a data-driven analysis of CLIP’s latent space using the MS COCO \cite{mscoco validation set, without relying on any prior assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We observe that both distributions of image and text follow the thin shell phenomenon \cite{klartag2023logarithmic, klartag2022bourgain , typical of high dimensional data, in which most of the mass concentrates within some range from the mean.}

elements.sent-2411-025: A known phenomenon in unsupervised contrastive learning is the existence of false negatives \cite{byun2022grit, li2022blip, yang2022vision .}

elements.sent-2411-036: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We investigate the raw CLIP embedding showing each modality resides on a separable ellipsoid shell not centered at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-038: We analyze the advantages of such structure, allowing to control the sharpness of the response in contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-039: False negative cases are shown to benefit mostly from this embedding.

elements.sent-2411-140: In contrastive learning, a well known problem is the phenomenon of false negatives.
elements.sent-2411-141: Essentially, concepts which are not dedicated pairs have similar meaning.
elements.sent-2411-142: Thus, they do not represent well negative examples and should not be embedded far apart.
elements.sent-2411-143: This problem appears for both single and multi-modality cases.
elements.sent-2411-144: There have been several suggestions to solve this problem by introducing new learning procedures or proposing alternative contrastive losses \cite{byun2024mafa, chuang2020debiased .}
elements.sent-2411-145: In the case of CLIP, however, the training is performed with a contrastive loss which does not take into consideration directly the false negative problem.
elements.sent-2411-146: We claim that the issue is mitigated, to some extent, by a specialized embedding.
elements.sent-2411-147: In classification and segmentation, as uncertainty grows, the network yields softer values, representing the lower membership probability to the class.

elements.sent-2411-412: Let us define by $z^j$ the difference vector between a vector $v^j$ and the mean vector $\bar{v}$, that is $z^j = v^j - \bar{v}$.. Then, \begin{equation}\|v^j\|^2 = \|z^j + \bar{v}\|^2 = \|z^j\|^2 + 2 z^j \cdot \bar{v} + \|\bar{v}\|^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-413: In high dimensions, the inner product $ z^j \cdot \bar{v $ is small due to approximate orthogonality, so: \begin{equation}\|v^j\|^2 \approx \|z^j\|^2 + \mu_{norm}^2 \approx R^2 + \mu_{norm}^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Thus, the scalar factor $A$ in Equation~\eqref{eq:conf is given by: \begin{equation}A = \frac{\mu_{norm}}{\|v^j\|} \approx \frac{\mu_{norm}}{\sqrt{R^2 + \mu_{norm}^2}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-416: Empirically we know for Vit-B/32 that $\mu_{norm = 7.587$ and $R \approx 7.59$, thus the mathematical derivation state that $A^{-1 = \frac{\sqrt{7.59^2 + 7.587^2 {7.587 = 1.414$ For images and $A^{-1 = \frac{\sqrt{5.59^2 + 5.75^2 {5.75 = 1.4$, very close to the empirical observations (note that the correlation is reversed in the main paper).}}}}}}}}}
